skill.name: web-artifacts-builder
skill.description: Build complex Claude web artifacts with React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, and bundled shadcn/ui components.
skill.summary: Scaffold and bundle React-based Claude artifacts as single HTML files.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk hits, but the weak-cryptography and license URL matches are false positives from Apache license text, CSS names, and documentation links. The confirmed risks are medium: the skill uses shell scripts that install npm packages, create and modify project files, extract a local component archive, and remove build outputs.

medium_findings.title: Package Installation and Tool Execution
medium_findings.description: The initialization and bundling scripts run pnpm, npm, Parcel, and html-inline. This is expected for a web artifact builder, but it downloads packages and executes local tooling, so users should review dependencies before running it in sensitive environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package-manager commands are explicit and central to the skill workflow. I found no evidence of credential exfiltration or hidden command construction, so the concern is operational supply-chain risk.

medium_findings.title: Project File Writes and Build Cleanup
medium_findings.description: The scripts create configuration files, modify TypeScript settings, extract a component archive, and remove dist and bundle.html in the current project. This behavior is legitimate for scaffolding, but it can overwrite or delete local project artifacts if run in the wrong directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes and rm command are directly visible. The risk is bounded because paths are mostly project-relative and the project name is quoted, but user caution is still required.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak-Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The static high-risk weak-cryptography findings are not real cryptographic use. They match Apache license wording, Tailwind CSS color tokens, and descriptive skill text rather than hashing, encryption, or authentication logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the referenced lines shows no crypto API calls or security-sensitive algorithms. These are scanner keyword collisions.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL Matches Are Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to Apache license URLs, the shadcn/ui documentation link, and a shadcn schema URL. These do not send local data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static references in license, configuration, and documentation contexts. No request code or data upload is present at those locations.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Override Attempt
low_findings.description: The skill instructions tell agents how to scaffold, bundle, and optionally test artifacts. I found no text asking the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip review, change risk levels, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full skill instruction file was reviewed. It contains workflow and design guidance, not marketplace audit override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Build Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on bash scripts for project initialization and bundling. This pattern requires users to run local commands and should be treated as higher risk than a documentation-only skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly directs users to invoke shell scripts. The pattern is legitimate but materially increases execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Build Cleanup Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundling script removes dist and bundle.html before building. The paths are project-relative, but the command can delete existing local build artifacts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The rm command is direct and easy to verify. It is not broad filesystem deletion, but it can affect user files with those names.

content.value_statement: Complex Claude artifacts often need state, components, and a real build pipeline. This skill scaffolds React projects and bundles them into shareable single-file HTML artifacts.
actual_capabilities: Creates a React 18 and TypeScript artifact project with Vite.
Installs Tailwind CSS, Radix UI dependencies, lucide-react, and shadcn/ui support packages.
Configures Tailwind CSS, PostCSS, Vite path aliases, and TypeScript path aliases.
Extracts a bundled shadcn/ui component archive into the generated project.
Bundles a built React app into one self-contained HTML file.
Provides guidance for optional local testing with browser automation tools.
limitations: Requires Node.js 18 or newer and a working pnpm environment.
Downloads packages from npm registries during setup and bundling.
Best suited for complex artifacts, not simple static HTML snippets.
Does not validate generated application behavior or accessibility by itself.

use_cases.title: Prototype Interactive Product Demos
use_cases.description: Create polished React demos with stateful controls, dialogs, forms, and responsive UI components.

use_cases.title: Ship Internal Web Tools as Artifacts
use_cases.description: Build small operational tools that need TypeScript, component reuse, and a single shareable HTML output.

use_cases.title: Prepare Component-Rich Client Mockups
use_cases.description: Assemble Tailwind and shadcn/ui mockups that can be shared quickly in Claude conversations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Artifact Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-artifacts-builder to create a new React artifact project named dashboard-demo. Keep the first screen focused and functional.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Stateful Interface
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an inventory planning artifact with filters, editable rows, summary metrics, and shadcn/ui form controls.

prompt_templates.title: Bundle for Sharing
prompt_templates.prompt: Bundle the current React artifact into one HTML file and summarize the output file location and any build warnings.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Production-Style Prototype
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a multi-view analytics artifact with routing-like state, charts, command menu navigation, dialogs, and a polished responsive layout.

output_examples.input: Create a project for a sales pipeline artifact.
output_examples.output: A new React project is scaffolded with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui components, and configured path aliases.

output_examples.input: Bundle my finished artifact for Claude.
output_examples.output: The build creates a self-contained bundle.html file with application code, styles, and assets inlined.

output_examples.input: Add component-rich UI patterns to a prototype.
output_examples.output: The generated project can use buttons, dialogs, tables, forms, tabs, menus, and other bundled shadcn/ui components.

best_practices: Run the scripts in a new project directory or a disposable workspace.
Review installed dependencies before using the artifact with sensitive data.
Use this skill when the artifact needs React state, reusable components, or bundling.
anti_patterns: Do not run the bundling script from an unrelated project root.
Do not use this skill for simple single-file HTML that needs no build step.
Do not assume the generated artifact has been tested for accessibility or browser compatibility.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a React and TypeScript web artifact project and can bundle it into one HTML file.

faq.question: Which frontend stack does it use?
faq.answer: It uses React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Parcel, and shadcn/ui components.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. The setup and bundling scripts install packages with npm and pnpm.

faq.question: Is it safe to run in an existing project?
faq.answer: Use caution. The scripts write configuration files and the bundler removes dist and bundle.html.

faq.question: Can it build simple static artifacts?
faq.answer: It can, but it is designed for complex artifacts that need React and a build pipeline.

faq.question: Does it automatically test the finished artifact?
faq.answer: No. It mentions optional testing, but users must request or perform validation separately.
